narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 76.9% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile includes being in the lowest decile for Year 1 grades at your school, the lowest decile for Year 3 grades, an upper-middle family income, an LSAT score of 36, and an undergraduate GPA of 2.50—all while identifying as a white male planning full-time studies. These details stood out when compared to typical applicants, where Year 1 and Year 3 deciles average around the middle of the pack, LSAT scores hit about 36.7, and GPAs reach 3.21 on average. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your low Year 1 decile ranking, which pushed the prediction firmly toward expecting a bar failure. Next in influence was your LSAT score, just shy of the norm, which also nudged the outlook negatively. Your Year 3 decile followed closely as a key factor tilting things toward failure, with family income and undergraduate GPA adding further downward pressure in that order. Your racial background, gender, and full-time status had the least sway but still contributed slightly to the risk assessment. Overall, your early and later school deciles were the top concerns, far below what's typical for students who pass the bar. To boost your future chances, focus on realistic steps like retaking the LSAT to aim higher than average, building a stronger undergraduate record if possible through post-bac courses, and demonstrating grade improvement in any advanced studies. Improving those core academic markers could shift the model's view dramatically, as they're among the most changeable factors. Many students in similar spots have turned things around with targeted prep—consider bar-focused tutoring early on too. You're not defined by this prediction; with effort on these fronts, a stronger profile awaits.
